behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection, with an explanation of the correct concept.

questions.stem: The vertebral (spinal) canal is enclosed posteriorly by which bony structures?
questions.explanation: The vertebral canal is enclosed posteriorly by the dorsal (vertebral) arches; the bodies form the anterior wall.

questions.stem: The intervertebral foramen, through which spinal nerves exit, is formed by the:

options.A: Superior and inferior vertebral notches of adjacent pedicles

questions.explanation: The intervertebral foramen is formed by the superior and inferior vertebral notches of adjacent pedicles.

questions.stem: The carotid tubercle (of Chassaignac) is found on the transverse process of which vertebra?
questions.explanation: The carotid (Chassaignac) tubercle is on the transverse process of C6.

questions.stem: The median sacral crest is formed by the fused:
questions.explanation: The median sacral crest = fused spinous processes of the sacral vertebrae.

questions.stem: From superior to inferior, the three parts of the sternum are:
questions.explanation: Top to bottom: manubrium, body, xiphoid process.

questions.stem: Regarding the intervertebral discs, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The nucleus pulposus is rich in type I collagen and sits at the centre of the disc.
options.B: The intervertebral disc is vascularized into adulthood by branches of the segmental arteries.
options.C: Disc height is greatest in the cervical region and least in the lumbar region.
options.D: The annulus fibrosus fibres cross in an "X" pattern and are mainly type I collagen, while the nucleus pulposus is rich in proteoglycans and water.
options.E: The annulus fibrosus consists of concentric lamellae of type II collagen set at a 30° angle to the horizontal plane.

questions.explanation: The annulus fibrosus is made of crossed (X-pattern) lamellae of type I collagen; the nucleus pulposus is gelatinous, rich in water and proteoglycans. The other options err on collagen type, fibre angle, vascularity (the adult disc is avascular), or disc height (greatest lumbar, least cervical/thoracic).

questions.stem: Regarding the atlas (C1) and axis (C2), which statement is correct?

options.A: The alar ligament joins the dens of the axis to the occiput and mainly limits flexion.
options.B: The atlanto-axial joint is a trochoid (pivot) joint allowing head rotation, with the transverse ligament of the atlas holding the dens of the axis.
options.C: The atlas has a well-developed vertebral body and an odontoid process.
options.D: The axis has lateral masses that articulate with the occipital condyles.
options.E: The atlas has no perforated transverse process.

questions.explanation: The atlanto-axial joint is a trochoid (pivot) joint; the transverse ligament holds the dens against the anterior arch of C1. The atlas has no body or dens (those belong to the axis), and the alar ligament chiefly limits rotation.

questions.stem: What is the most distinctive feature of a typical cervical vertebra (C3–C6)?

options.A: A transverse foramen carrying the vertebral artery and the vertebral venous plexus.
options.B: A long, bifid, downward-sloping spinous process.
options.D: Costal facets on the body and the transverse process.
options.E: A superior articular process facing backward and upward.

questions.explanation: The transverse foramen (carrying the vertebral artery, except at C7, plus the venous plexus and sympathetic fibres) is the defining feature of a cervical vertebra. The bifid spinous process and kidney-shaped body are features too, but the transverse foramen is the single most distinctive.

questions.stem: Regarding the thoraco-lumbar junction and the spinal curvatures, which statement is correct?

options.A: The cervical and lumbar lordoses are primary curvatures present at birth.
options.B: The lumbar lordosis is absent in the newborn and develops with walking.
options.C: The thoraco-lumbar junction (T11–L2) is an important biomechanical transition zone where facet orientation changes, raising the risk of fracture and disc herniation.
options.D: All spinal curvatures are primary curvatures.
options.E: The thoracic kyphosis is a secondary curvature that develops once a child begins to sit.

questions.explanation: The thoraco-lumbar junction (T11–L2) is a biomechanical transition where facet orientation shifts (coronal → sagittal), making it a site of weakness. Primary curvatures = thoracic and sacral kyphoses (present at birth); secondary = cervical and lumbar lordoses (develop with posture).

questions.stem: How many bones are there in one upper limb (including the pectoral girdle)?
questions.explanation: Each upper limb has 30 bones (clavicle + scapula + humerus + radius + ulna + 8 carpals + 5 metacarpals + 14 phalanges).

questions.stem: Teres major attaches to which feature of the scapula?
questions.explanation: Teres major attaches at the inferior angle of the scapula (origin), running to the crest of the lesser tubercle.

questions.stem: Which three rotator cuff muscles attach to the greater tubercle of the humerus?
questions.explanation: Greater tubercle = supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor (superior, middle, inferior facets). Subscapularis is the lone lesser-tubercle muscle.

questions.stem: Regarding the clavicle, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The clavicle articulates with the sternum medially and the acromion laterally by synovial joints.
options.B: The clavicle is the only long bone that ossifies by intramembranous ossification.
options.C: The conoid and trapezoid tubercles lie on the inferior surface of the lateral end and give attachment to the coracoclavicular ligaments.
options.D: The most frequent clavicular fracture occurs at the medial third.
options.E: The clavicle is a long bone that ossifies by endochondral ossification only.

questions.explanation: The conoid and trapezoid tubercles sit on the inferior surface of the lateral clavicle for the coracoclavicular ligaments. The clavicle ossifies mainly by intramembranous ossification (so E is wrong; B is true in isolation but the question asks the most accurate attachment statement). The commonest fracture is at the middle third, and both its joints are synovial but that is not the most precise/complete statement here.

questions.stem: Regarding the humerus, which statement is correct?

options.A: The olecranon fossa lies on the anterior surface of the medial epicondyle.
options.B: The radial (spiral) groove lies on the anterior surface of the shaft.
options.C: The medial epicondyle is the origin site of the forearm extensor muscles.
options.D: The greater tubercle gives attachment to supraspinatus, infraspinatus and teres minor.
options.E: The intertubercular (bicipital) groove separates the greater and lesser tubercles and holds the long head of biceps tendon.

questions.explanation: The intertubercular (bicipital) groove separates the greater and lesser tubercles and holds the long head of biceps tendon. The greater tubercle takes supraspinatus/infraspinatus/teres minor; the radial groove is postero-lateral; the medial epicondyle is the flexor origin; the olecranon fossa is posterior.

questions.stem: The ilium, ischium, and pubis fuse with one another at the:
questions.explanation: The three hip-bone components fuse at the acetabulum (via the triradiate cartilage).

questions.stem: The obturator foramen is formed by which two bones?
questions.explanation: The obturator foramen is bounded by the pubis and ischium.

questions.stem: The lunate surface of the acetabulum is best described as the:
questions.explanation: The lunate (crescentic) surface is the weight-bearing articular surface of the acetabulum; the central acetabular fossa is non-articular.

questions.stem: The intertrochanteric line:

options.C: Lies on the medial femoral condyle
options.D: Is the attachment for the gluteus maximus

questions.explanation: The intertrochanteric line connects the trochanters anteriorly; the intertrochanteric crest connects them posteriorly.

questions.stem: The intercondylar fossa of the femur houses the:
questions.explanation: The intercondylar fossa houses the cruciate ligaments.

questions.stem: Regarding the acetabulum, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The acetabulum faces laterally, anteriorly and inferiorly.
options.B: The lunate surface is covered with hyaline cartilage and articulates with the femoral head; the transverse acetabular ligament closes the inferior part of the acetabulum.
options.C: The acetabular fossa is cartilage-covered and takes part in the joint.
options.D: The ligament of the femoral head (ligamentum teres) attaches to the acetabular fossa.
options.E: The acetabulum is formed by fusion of the three bones of the hip bone: ilium, ischium and pubis.

questions.explanation: The lunate surface is the hyaline-cartilage-covered articular part; the transverse acetabular ligament closes the acetabular notch inferiorly. The acetabular fossa is NOT cartilage-covered (it holds fat + ligamentum teres) and does not articulate. Options A, D and E are individually true but B is the most complete/precise statement.

questions.stem: Regarding the differences between the male and female pelvis, which statement is correct?

options.A: The male pelvis is wider and more flared than the female pelvis.
options.B: The ischial spine is more prominent and pointed in the female.
options.C: The subpubic angle is larger in the male (about 70–75°) than in the female (about 90–100°).
options.D: The pelvic inlet is wider and rounder in the female, whereas it is narrower and heart-shaped in the male.
options.E: The sacrum is shorter and broader in the male.

questions.explanation: The pelvic inlet is wider, rounder and larger in the female (obstetric adaptation); narrower and heart-shaped in the male. The subpubic angle is larger in the female (~90–100°) and the female pelvis is the wider, more flared one.

questions.stem: Regarding the obturator canal and the ligaments of the pelvis, which statement is correct?

options.A: The sacrotuberous ligament is an intrinsic ligament of the sacro-iliac joint.
options.B: The iliofemoral ligament is the weakest ligament of the hip joint.
options.C: The greater sciatic foramen is divided by the sacrospinous ligament.
options.D: The pubofemoral ligament limits extension of the hip.
options.E: The obturator canal is formed by the obturator groove of the hip bone and the obturator membrane; it transmits the obturator nerve and vessels.

questions.explanation: The obturator canal (obturator groove + obturator membrane) transmits the obturator nerve and vessels. The sacrotuberous ligament is extra-articular; the iliofemoral ligament is the strongest hip ligament.

questions.stem: How many facial bones are there, and which unpaired bone is among them?
questions.explanation: There are 14 facial bones; the vomer (and the mandible) are the unpaired ones.

questions.stem: The pterion is the junction primarily of the parietal bone and the greater wing of the sphenoid. What lies deep to it?
questions.explanation: The pterion overlies the anterior division of the middle meningeal artery — a clinically vulnerable spot.

questions.stem: The middle cranial fossa is formed by the sphenoid and the:
questions.explanation: The middle cranial fossa is formed by the sphenoid plus the two temporal bones.

questions.stem: The foramen spinosum transmits the:
questions.explanation: The foramen spinosum transmits the middle meningeal artery (foramen ovale transmits CN V3).

questions.stem: The posterior cranial fossa is formed mainly by the:
questions.explanation: The posterior cranial fossa is formed mainly by the occipital bone.

questions.stem: The internal acoustic meatus and the jugular foramen open into which cranial fossa?
questions.explanation: Both the internal acoustic meatus and the jugular foramen open into the posterior cranial fossa.

questions.stem: Regarding the foramina of the skull base, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The foramen spinosum transmits the mandibular nerve (V3).
options.B: The carotid canal transmits the internal carotid artery and the vagus nerve.
options.C: The jugular foramen transmits the glossopharyngeal (IX), vagus (X) and accessory (XI) nerves, plus the internal jugular vein.
options.D: The foramen rotundum transmits the maxillary nerve (V2) and the middle meningeal artery.
options.E: The foramen ovale transmits the mandibular nerve (V3), the middle meningeal artery and sometimes the maxillary artery.

questions.explanation: The jugular foramen transmits CN IX, X, XI and the internal jugular vein. The foramen ovale carries V3 (± accessory meningeal artery, NOT the middle meningeal — that's the foramen spinosum); the carotid canal carries the ICA + sympathetic/carotid plexus, not the vagus; foramen rotundum carries V2 only.

questions.stem: Which is the correct full list of the seven bones forming the bony orbit?

options.A: Frontal, sphenoid, ethmoid, lacrimal, maxilla, zygomatic, palatine
options.B: Frontal, sphenoid, ethmoid, nasal, maxilla, zygomatic, vomer
options.C: Frontal, parietal, ethmoid, lacrimal, maxilla, zygomatic, palatine
options.D: Frontal, sphenoid, temporal, lacrimal, maxilla, zygomatic, palatine

questions.explanation: Orbit (7 bones): Frontal, Sphenoid, Ethmoid, Lacrimal, Maxilla, Zygomatic, Palatine.

questions.stem: The lacrimal groove (fossa for the lacrimal sac) is formed by the lacrimal bone and the:

options.B: Orbital plate of the frontal bone

questions.explanation: The lacrimal groove = lacrimal bone + frontal process of the maxilla.

questions.stem: The nasolacrimal canal/duct drains tears into which structure?
questions.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct drains into the inferior meatus.

questions.stem: The inferior nasal concha is best classified as:
questions.explanation: The inferior nasal concha is a separate facial bone (unlike the superior/middle conchae, which are part of the ethmoid).

questions.stem: The stylomastoid foramen transmits which cranial nerve?
questions.explanation: The stylomastoid foramen transmits the facial nerve (CN VII).

questions.stem: Regarding the sphenoid bone seen externally, which statement is correct?

options.A: The lesser wings of the sphenoid form the roof of the orbit.
options.B: The sphenoid bone articulates with every cranial bone except the occipital.
options.C: The greater wings of the sphenoid form part of the temporal fossa and contain the foramen ovale and foramen spinosum.
options.D: The body of the sphenoid contains the sella turcica and the sphenoidal sinuses.
options.E: The pterygoid process is formed by the lateral and medial plates and attaches to the maxilla.

questions.explanation: The greater wings contain the foramina ovale and spinosum and form part of the temporal fossa. The lesser wings do form the orbital roof, but the question targets the external/temporal-fossa relationship; the sphenoid DOES articulate with the occipital.

questions.stem: Regarding the occipital bone seen externally, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The foramen magnum is bounded anteriorly by the clivus and posteriorly by the occipital squama.
options.B: The pharyngeal tubercle lies on the external surface of the basilar part.
options.C: The occipital condyles articulate with the lateral masses of the atlas.
options.D: The superior nuchal line gives attachment to the trapezius and the sternocleidomastoid muscles.
options.E: The occipital bone does not contribute to the posterior cranial fossa.

questions.explanation: The occipital condyles articulate with the lateral masses of the atlas (atlanto-occipital joint). The superior nuchal line gives attachment chiefly to trapezius and SCM, but the condylar articulation is the fundamental, always-true relationship; the occipital clearly forms the posterior fossa.

questions.stem: Regarding the occipitofrontalis (frontal belly), which statement is correct?

options.A: It belongs to the group of muscles of mastication.
options.B: It is a deep muscle of the face.
options.C: Its insertion on the galea aponeurotica lets it act on the scalp.
options.D: It is innervated by the trigeminal nerve (V).
options.E: It is innervated by the facial nerve (VII) and raises the eyebrows, producing expressions of surprise.

questions.explanation: The frontal belly of occipitofrontalis is a muscle of facial expression, innervated by the facial nerve (VII); it raises the eyebrows. It is not a muscle of mastication (those take V3).

questions.stem: Regarding the buccinator muscle, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: It forms the lateral wall of the oral cavity, is innervated by the facial nerve (VII), and aids mastication by holding the cheek against the teeth.
options.B: It is innervated by the mandibular nerve (V3).
options.C: It belongs to the muscles of mastication.
options.D: It inserts on the angle of the mandible.
options.E: It is an elevator of the mandible.

questions.explanation: The buccinator is a muscle of facial expression (VII), forming the lateral wall of the mouth and holding food against the teeth in chewing. It is NOT a muscle of mastication (masseter, temporalis, pterygoids — V3).

questions.stem: Regarding the deep muscles of the oral region, which statement is correct?

options.A: They are innervated by the trigeminal nerve.
options.B: The mentalis is an elevator of the upper lip.
options.C: These muscles belong to the muscles of mastication.
options.D: The mentalis and the incisivus muscles (superior and inferior) are deep muscles acting on the lower lip and chin, innervated by the facial nerve.
options.E: The superior incisivus muscle inserts on the mandible.

questions.explanation: The mentalis and incisivus muscles are deep muscles of the oral region innervated by the facial nerve (VII), acting on the lower lip/chin. They are not muscles of mastication and not trigeminal-innervated.

questions.stem: The external intercostal muscles are active during which phase, and their fibres run in which direction?
questions.explanation: External intercostals = inspiratory; fibres run downward and forward.

questions.stem: The innermost intercostals are separated from the internal intercostals by the:
questions.explanation: The intercostal neurovascular bundle separates the innermost from the internal intercostals.

questions.stem: Transversus thoracis is continuous inferiorly with which abdominal muscle?
questions.explanation: Transversus thoracis is continuous with transversus abdominis.

questions.stem: The right dome of the diaphragm is 2–3 cm higher than the left because of the:
questions.explanation: The right dome sits higher because of the liver beneath it.

questions.stem: All muscle fibres of the diaphragm insert onto the:
questions.explanation: All diaphragmatic fibres converge on the central tendon.

questions.stem: The right crus of the diaphragm arises from which vertebrae (and the left crus from which)?
questions.explanation: Right crus = L1–L3; left crus = L1–L2.

questions.stem: The caval (IVC) foramen lies at which level and transmits what?

options.C: T8; IVC + right phrenic terminal branches

questions.explanation: Caval foramen at T8 transmits the IVC + terminal branches of the right phrenic nerve. (T8/T10/T12 — even numbers ascending.)

questions.stem: The aortic hiatus lies at T12 and transmits the aorta, the azygos vein, and the:
questions.explanation: Aortic hiatus (T12): aorta, thoracic duct, azygos vein.

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four scapulothoracic muscles?
questions.explanation: The four scapulothoracic muscles are pec major, pec minor, serratus anterior, subclavius — NOT latissimus dorsi.

questions.stem: Pectoralis minor inserts onto the:
questions.explanation: Pectoralis minor inserts on the coracoid process.

questions.stem: The external oblique fibres run in which direction?
questions.explanation: External oblique fibres run downward and medially ("hands in pockets"); internal oblique runs the opposite way (up-and-medial).

questions.stem: In males, the lower fibres of which muscle form the cremaster?
questions.explanation: Internal oblique's lower fibres form the cremaster muscle in males.

questions.stem: Regarding the intercostal muscles, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The external intercostals are expiratory muscles.
options.B: The internal and innermost (intimi) intercostals are separated by the intercostal neurovascular bundle.
options.C: The transversus thoracis is an inspiratory muscle.
options.D: The innermost intercostals lie superficial (external) to the neurovascular bundle.
options.E: The external intercostals are inspiratory muscles running from the upper ribs to the lower ribs in a postero-anterior direction.

questions.explanation: The internal and innermost intercostals are separated by the intercostal neurovascular bundle. External intercostals are inspiratory; internal/innermost are expiratory; transversus thoracis is expiratory. (Option E states the right phase but the wrong fibre direction — external fibres run downward-and-forward, antero-inferiorly.)

questions.stem: Regarding the diaphragm, which statement is correct?

options.A: The phrenic nerve innervates the diaphragm and arises from roots C4–C6.
options.B: The caval (vena cava) foramen lies at the level of T10.
options.C: The diaphragm is innervated by the vagus nerve.
options.D: The Bochdalek and Morgagni triangles are areas of weakness in the central part of the diaphragm.
options.E: The right crus inserts on the vertebral bodies of L1–L3 and the left crus on L1–L2; the esophageal hiatus is at T10 and the aortic hiatus at T12.

questions.explanation: Right crus L1–L3, left crus L1–L2; esophageal hiatus T10, aortic hiatus T12. The phrenic nerve is C3–C5 (not C4–C6); the caval foramen is at T8 (not T10); Bochdalek/Morgagni triangles are postero-lateral/antero-medial weak spots, not central.

questions.stem: The extrinsic back muscles are called "immigrant muscles" because they:

options.B: Represent upper-limb muscles that migrated to the back in development

questions.explanation: "Immigrant" muscles are developmentally upper-limb muscles that migrated onto the back (hence ventral-ramus innervation).

questions.stem: Intrinsic (true) back muscles are innervated by the ____, whereas extrinsic back muscles are innervated by ____:
questions.explanation: Intrinsic = dorsal (posterior) rami; extrinsic = ventral branches. This is the single most reliable discriminator.

questions.stem: What separates the intrinsic deep back muscles from the overlying extrinsic muscles?
questions.explanation: The thoracolumbar fascia separates the deep intrinsic group from the superficial extrinsic muscles.

questions.stem: Motor innervation of the trapezius is by the:
questions.explanation: Trapezius motor supply = spinal accessory nerve (CN XI), with C3–C4 proprioception.

questions.stem: Latissimus dorsi inserts onto the ____, whereas pectoralis major inserts onto the ____:

options.A: Crest of greater tubercle; intertubercular sulcus
options.B: Intertubercular (bicipital) sulcus; crest of the greater tubercle

questions.explanation: Latissimus dorsi → intertubercular (bicipital) sulcus (floor); pectoralis major → crest of the greater tubercle (lateral lip). Don't swap these.

questions.stem: Levator scapulae originates from the transverse processes of which vertebrae, and is innervated by which nerve?
questions.explanation: Levator scapulae = C1–C4 transverse processes, dorsal scapular nerve; elevates the scapula.

questions.stem: Rhomboid minor arises from ____ and rhomboid major from ____:
questions.explanation: Rhomboid minor = C7–T1; rhomboid major = T2–T5 (minor is higher, smaller).

questions.stem: Serratus posterior inferior acts as a synergist of:
questions.explanation: Serratus posterior inferior depresses ribs 9–12 → expiration synergist. (Serratus posterior superior = inspiration.)

questions.stem: The transversospinales (deep layer) consist of:
questions.explanation: Transversospinales (deep layer) = semispinalis, multifidus, rotatores (run transverse → spinous).

questions.stem: In addition to extension, the semispinalis also:

options.B: Contralaterally rotates the head and vertebral column

questions.explanation: Semispinalis extends AND contralaterally rotates the head and vertebral column.

questions.stem: Which deep intrinsic muscle is strongly developed in the lumbar region, ascends 2–4 segments, and lies deep to the semispinalis?
questions.explanation: Multifidus: deep to semispinalis, strong in the lumbar region, ascends 2–4 vertebral segments.

questions.stem: The four suboccipital muscles are all innervated by the:
questions.explanation: All four suboccipital muscles are innervated by the suboccipital nerve.

questions.stem: Regarding the classification of the back muscles, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: The intrinsic (deep) back muscles are innervated by ventral branches of the spinal nerves.
options.B: The trapezius is an intrinsic back muscle.
options.C: The latissimus dorsi is innervated by the accessory nerve (XI).
options.D: The erector spinae muscles are extrinsic muscles.
options.E: The extrinsic (superficial) back muscles are innervated by ventral branches of the spinal nerves and are mainly "immigrant" muscles of the shoulder girdle.

questions.explanation: The extrinsic (superficial) muscles — trapezius, latissimus dorsi, levator scapulae, rhomboids — are "immigrant" shoulder-girdle muscles innervated by ventral branches. The intrinsic muscles take dorsal rami. Trapezius is extrinsic (accessory nerve); latissimus = thoracodorsal nerve.

questions.stem: Regarding the trapezius, which statement is correct?

options.A: All fibres of the trapezius have the same action on the scapula.
options.B: The trapezius is innervated solely by the accessory nerve (XI).
options.C: The upper fibres elevate and laterally rotate the scapula, the middle fibres retract it, and the lower fibres depress and laterally rotate it; innervated by the accessory nerve (XI) plus sensory fibres from C3–C4.
options.D: The lower fibres of the trapezius elevate the scapula.
options.E: The trapezius belongs to the intrinsic back muscles.

questions.explanation: Upper fibres elevate + laterally rotate the scapula, middle fibres retract, lower fibres depress + laterally rotate; motor = accessory nerve (XI), proprioception C3–C4. The fibres have different actions, and trapezius is extrinsic.

questions.stem: Which four muscles make up the rotator cuff?
questions.explanation: Rotator cuff = SITS: Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis.

questions.stem: Which rotator cuff muscle inserts on the lesser tubercle, and what is its action?
questions.explanation: Subscapularis is the only cuff muscle on the lesser tubercle; it medially rotates the arm.

questions.stem: The two heads of biceps brachii arise from the:

options.A: Long head = coracoid process; short head = supraglenoid tubercle
options.B: Long head = supraglenoid tubercle; short head = coracoid process
options.C: Long head = infraglenoid tubercle; short head = coracoid process
options.D: Both heads from the coracoid process

questions.explanation: Long head = supraglenoid tubercle; short head = coracoid process.

questions.stem: Biceps brachii inserts via its common tendon onto the:
questions.explanation: Biceps inserts on the radial tuberosity (plus the bicipital aponeurosis).

questions.stem: The long head of triceps brachii arises from the infraglenoid tubercle; all three heads insert onto the:
questions.explanation: Long head of triceps = infraglenoid tubercle; all three heads insert on the olecranon.

questions.stem: Regarding the pectoralis major, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: It is innervated solely by the lateral pectoral nerve.
options.B: It has three heads (clavicular, sternocostal and abdominal); it adducts, flexes and medially rotates the humerus; it is innervated by the lateral and medial pectoral nerves.
options.C: The clavicular head is the most powerful for adduction.
options.D: It inserts on the crest of the greater tubercle of the humerus.
options.E: It is a muscle of the posterior compartment of the arm.

questions.explanation: Pectoralis major: three heads (clavicular, sternocostal, abdominal); adducts/flexes/medially rotates the humerus; dual innervation by lateral + medial pectoral nerves. It inserts on the crest of the greater tubercle (option D is true in isolation but B is the most complete statement).

questions.stem: Regarding the rotator cuff, which statement is correct?

options.A: The supraspinatus is the chief lateral rotator of the humerus.
options.B: The subscapularis is a lateral rotator.
options.C: All cuff muscles are innervated by the suprascapular nerve.
options.D: The rotator cuff does not contribute to shoulder stability.
options.E: It is formed by the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor and subscapularis (SITS); these stabilize the humeral head in the glenoid cavity and are innervated by branches of the brachial plexus.

questions.explanation: The cuff (SITS) stabilizes the humeral head in the glenoid. Innervation: supra-/infraspinatus = suprascapular; teres minor = axillary; subscapularis = upper + lower subscapular — i.e., branches of the brachial plexus, not all suprascapular. Supraspinatus = abductor (not lateral rotator); subscapularis = medial rotator.

questions.stem: The levator ani (pelvic diaphragm) consists of pubococcygeus, puborectalis, and:
questions.explanation: Levator ani = pubococcygeus + puborectalis + iliococcygeus. Coccygeus is separate.

questions.stem: Which anterior thigh muscle is the longest muscle in the body (ASIS → pes anserinus), innervated by the femoral nerve?
questions.explanation: Sartorius is the longest muscle in the body (ASIS → pes anserinus); femoral nerve.

questions.stem: Which quadriceps head is the only one to cross the hip joint?
questions.explanation: Rectus femoris is the only quadriceps head crossing the hip (origin AIIS) — flexes hip + extends knee.

questions.stem: The three muscles forming the pes anserinus are:
questions.explanation: Pes anserinus = Sartorius, Gracilis, semiTendinosus ("Say Grace before Tea").

questions.stem: The femoral vessels pass from the thigh into the popliteal fossa through the:
questions.explanation: The femoral vessels pass through the adductor hiatus of adductor magnus to become the popliteal vessels.

questions.stem: How do biceps femoris actions on the flexed leg differ from the other two hamstrings?

options.A: It medially rotates; the others laterally rotate
options.B: It laterally rotates the flexed leg; the others medially rotate

questions.explanation: Biceps femoris (lateral hamstring → fibular head) laterally rotates the flexed leg; semitendinosus and semimembranosus (medial hamstrings) medially rotate it.
